So, as I mentioned, macro weather model based on everything we can currently observe from a Bayesian prior standpoint, uh says things are fine. Uh you got a modest tailwind in the growth cycle. You got a uh meaningful tailwind in the monetary policy cycle. You got a modest tailwind in the fiscal policy cycle. You got a modest tailwind in the liquidity cycle. You only have a modest headwind in the position cycle and neutral on inflation right now. So, you know, if this persists, we're going to be fine. The issue we're highlighting from a fundamental standpoint is that this part, monetary policy cycle, might inflect uh to a negative trend because this stays sticky or gets worse. Brent crude oil has a weak negative impulse at minus 25% 3-month annualized. The 5-year 5-year inflation swap rate strong negative impulse at 2.41% and then the potential short run potential headline CPI estimate strong positive impulse at 2.9%. Looking at the monetary policy cycle got a meaningful tailwind right there weak easing impulse in the Fed funds rate at 3.75%. The 2-year nominal Treasury yield Fed funds rate spread that's a strong tightening impulse at 21 positive 21 basis points. Fed Treasury holdings to marketable Treasury debt ratio that's a weak easing impulse at 14%. Bank reserves to commercial bank assets ratio that's a weak easing impulse at 12% and then you got a SOFR OIS spread that's a strong easing impulse at minus three basis points. Um my fiscal policy cycle we got a modest tailwind in the fiscal policy cycle strong 12-month sovereign fiscal balance to GDP ratio that's a weak easing impulse at minus 5.5%. The trailing 12-month federal revenue, that's a strong easing impulse at minus 2%. The trailing 12-month federal expenditures, that's a weak tightening impulse at 0%. Bills to marketable Treasury debt ratio, that's a weak tightening impulse at 22%. And then the Treasury general account balance to bank reserves ratio, that's a weak easing impulse at 29%. Liquidity cycle, minus tailwind there, you got a strong positive impulse in global liquidity and US liquidity. We got a weak tightening impulse in the move index. You got a weak easing impulse in the 10-year US Treasury term premium at 69 basis points and the strong tightening impulse in the US dollar index at 99. And then finally positioning cycle, got a modest headwind there. Three of the five positioning The top three of the top five positioning cycle indicators are breaching their bubble peak means when you compare to the everything bubble peak in Jan 2022, the housing bubble peak in October of '07 and the dot-com bubble peak in March of 2000. So, again, as I say, if we if we just stay status quo, the market's going to bubble. It's going to continue to bubble. However, we may not stay status quo. Big known unknown that we're going to start to get signals from with regards to the US monetary policy cycle, the US inflation cycle may continue to may disappoint investors over the medium term as well. That's At least according to our grid model, that's not the highest probability outcome. The grid model sees inflation actually slowing faster than what consensus currently expects consensus currently expects inflation to be pretty sticky through let's call it early fall and then start to break down in Q4. Our model The grid model is suggesting that that process may start to happen sooner. And then obviously the grid model continues to be above consensus on growth as well. So, those are two very favorable dynamics. If this happens, then you don't have to worry about the Fed. And if you don't have to worry about the Fed, then let's giddy up. So, yeah. It's either 1999 or It's either 1998 or 1999. So, I know those are two very disparate outcomes and it's a very bimodal distribution right now. What we're essentially arguing from a risk management standpoint is that we have a bimodal distribution where, you know, Goldilocks is probably the modal outcome from a market regime perspective over the over the medium term between now and year end if if the Fed looks through inflation and inflation will be the modal outcome from market regime perspective this summer and and into the fall if if the Fed chooses not to look through the current bout of accelerating above target inflation. Right now, consensus is pretty much betting on this being the you know, the overwhelming highest probability outcome with very little probability associated with this and so that's the risk from a macro risk management standpoint. You got to be focused on, okay, what are the signs that we're actually going actually the market's going to move to price that in so that ultimately when Kashkari and Mester start signaling that if that becomes the outcome, you feel confident enough to just say, okay, yeah, I'm taking down risk side. This is time to head for the exits at least for the time being. There's no credible case to be made that the Fed should look through the strong positive impulses in the growth rates of base money, narrow money, and broad money, especially not amid cyclically and structurally elevated levels of M1 and M2 money velocity. The only saving grace here is that M1 and M2 are growing at trend levels. Accelerations beyond these thresholds would shut down the look through narrative. So, we got a strong positive impulse in base money growth at 5%. That number is way above trend. We got a strong positive impulse in narrow money growth at 7.1%. That number is right at trend. We got a strong positive impulse in broad money growth at 6.7%. That number is right at trend. And then we got a cyclically and structurally elevated money velocity, M1 money velocity, and M2 money velocity. So, we're kind of at these kind of critical thresholds from a growth rate level perspective that if we you know, we go beyond these levels, these we go into an above trend growth rate of narrow and broad money growth at the same time we have a high degree of money velocity. I mean, this is this is how inflation accidents happen when the Fed is asleep at the wheel and these types of dynamics are occurring in an economy that is being run nominally hot. It's deliberately being run nominally hot. That is what paradigm C is. And so, you know, you have expansionary fiscal policy, you know, in order to just, you know, keep the inflation, you know, targeting, you know, in check, you need to have contractionary monetary policy. If you don't have contraction and monetary policy, then you're just going to inflation's going to be higher than what the Fed is supposed to target suggests. And I'm not saying they actually want 2% inflation. We know they don't. We're all frogs being boiled alive in a pot of financial repression and monetary debasement. Their job is to boil us alive without people exiting the bond market in mass. That's their That's their job. They're trying to engineer a much better, you know, dynamics from the perspective of the US the sovereign the part of any balance in the treasury bond market. By by basically running, you know, financially oppressing us and then using monetary debt monetization, which is monetary debasement. That's exactly what's happening. We are being financially oppressed currently right now and maybe being financially oppressed indefinitely. And so it's the Fed's job to do that in ways that don't spook the bond market. You know, that's so that's the game. That's the dance that they're trying to play right here. I don't think they actually want 2% inflation. If they did, their policy choices would have been a whole lot different in recent years. Reminder, inflation is by far the most lagging indicator of the business cycle and is unlikely to durably return to the Fed's arbitrary 2% target without an actual recession. So we know inflation is by far the most lagging indicator business cycle. It breaks down, let's call it 12 to 15 months after recession starts. So unless the Fed is willing to put the top part of the K into the same recession that it put the bottom part of the K in and is still in according to all the the delinquency rates and charge-off rates, you know, if the Fed is not willing to put the top part of the K into that same recession that they put the bottom part of the K in, then inflation is not going to break down durably below trend in a way to get 2% inflation. We know this. I've been saying this for almost 4 years, for actually 4 plus years now. Reminder, the cycle inflation model has accurately forecasted an equilibrium core PCE rate in the high 2s, low 3s since January 2022. We're still 3.1% right now. You know, when we weight the model 50% AI, let's say let's say AI does cause a disinflationary productivity boom, we only get to 2.4%. So we have to have a disinflationary productivity boom from AI just to get the equilibrium core PCE rate to a level that is slightly higher than the Fed's 2% target. So, the Fed's going to lose on inflation no matter what. I know it, you know it, they know it. It's their job to not tell us they know it, and it's our there's their job to boil us alive in that pot, um you know, the frogs that we are, uh without spooking the Treasury bond market. So, again, you know, it's a dance, and it's our job as investors to dance with them and and and front-run their their their their pivots in this dance so we can, you know, make stay on the right side of market risk.
